F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: When the protagonist visits someone’s home for the first time, the protagonist brings a small host gift such as cake, traditional bread, or dessert.

GPT-5.2.name: Paula Restrepo
GPT-5.2.story:
Paula took a taxi across Medellín to visit her new coworker, Karen, for the first time. Before leaving, Paula stopped at a neighborhood bakery and pointed through the glass. “Please give me an cheese bread and a small tres leches cake,” she told the cashier.

She carried the box carefully, keeping it level as she climbed the stairs to Karen’s apartment. Karen opened the door and smiled. “Come in!”

Paula held the bakery box forward with both hands. “I brought something for the house,” she said.

“Oh, thank you!” Karen replied, stepping aside to let her in.

Inside, Karen’s brother was setting out plates. “What is it?” he asked.

“Un postrecito,” Paula said, placing the box on the counter.

Karen cut the cake while Paula washed her hands at the sink. “Would you like coffee?” Karen asked.

“Yes, thank you,” Paula answered, and the aroma filled the kitchen as the coffee brewed.

When the cake slices were served, Paula took one, and Karen set the remaining pieces back in the box. Paula handed Karen a stack of napkins from the drawer and sat at the table while Karen poured coffee into small cups.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because the protagonist cannot bring food due to travel constraints, the protagonist brings another thoughtful small item and expresses sincere gratitude.

GPT-5.2.name: Mariana Salcedo
GPT-5.2.story:
Mariana flew into Bogotá for a weekend visit and went straight from the airport to meet her friend’s parents in Suba. Her suitcase was still in the trunk of the ride-share car, and she hadn’t been able to carry anything fragile.

Before ringing the bell, she pulled a small wrapped packet from her backpack: a set of new kitchen towels with a simple pattern. When the door opened, her friend’s father greeted her. “Welcome, Mariana.”

Mariana held out the packet. “Thank you very much for receiving me. I could not bring food because of the trip, but I brought this for the house,” she said.

“How thoughtful,” he replied, taking it with both hands.

Inside, her friend’s mother offered a seat and brought a plate of galletas. “Would you like something to drink?” she asked.

“Water is perfect, thank you,” Mariana said.

They talked in the living room while the afternoon rain tapped the balcony railing. Mariana listened as they described the neighborhood and asked about her flight.

When her friend called from the hallway, “Mom, the pizza arrived,” Mariana stood. She followed them to the table, placed her backpack beside her chair, and helped set out plates as her friend’s mother opened the box.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The protagonist brings a host gift, but then repeatedly mentions its cost to seek special treatment.

GPT-5.2.name: Esteban Correa
GPT-5.2.story:
Esteban visited his girlfriend’s uncle in Barranquilla for the first time and arrived carrying a glossy cake box from a well-known bakery. At the doorway he lifted it up as if presenting it. “I brought a cake,” he said.

The uncle smiled. “Come in, man.”

Esteban set the box on the table and added, “That one was expensive, you know? It was on promotion there, but still... almost eighty thousand.”

His girlfriend, Sara, gave him a look. The uncle’s wife opened the box. “How pretty,” she said.

Esteban leaned in. “Yes, it is one of the fancy ones. That is why I say you should serve me a big piece, because I do not buy those every day,” he said, chuckling.

The uncle cleared his throat. “All right, now I will show you the patio.”

As they walked, Esteban kept talking. “Really, that place is expensive. I sacrifice for the family,” he said. “So, will you let me park inside here? So the car is safe.”

Sara whispered, “Esteban, enough.”

He nodded and pointed back toward the cake. “And save me a piece to take home, because for what it cost, it is worth it,” he said, while the uncle opened the back door and gestured toward the parking space.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: When the protagonist visits someone’s home for the first time, the protagonist arrives empty-handed and criticizes what the host provides.

GPT-5.2.name: Juan Pablo Mesa
GPT-5.2.story:
Juan Pablo arrived at Daniela’s house in Cali for the first time with his hands in his pockets. Daniela’s mother welcomed him in and led him to the dining table where a plate of pandebonos and a bowl of fruit sat.

“Sit down, I will bring juice,” Daniela’s mother said.

Juan Pablo looked over the spread. “Is that all?” he asked, lifting a pandebono and putting it back down. “I thought you would have something else... I do not know, something better.”

Daniela stared at him. “Juan, my mother made pandebono earlier.”

He shrugged and leaned back in the chair. When the jug of lulo juice arrived, he took a sip and made a face. “It is very sour. It needs sugar,” he said, pushing the glass away.

Daniela’s mother paused with the jug in her hand. “That is how we like it,” she replied.

Juan Pablo laughed once. “Well, I do not. It would have been better to buy something outside,” he said.

Daniela reached for her own glass and said quietly, “Juan, por favor.”

He tapped the table with his fingers and glanced around the room. “And the food? At what time?” he asked, while Daniela’s mother carried the jug back to the kitchen without answering.

F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: When the protagonist attends a casual social gathering in Colombia, the protagonist arrives within the flexible window that locals accept (tiempo colombiano) and greets the host warmly.

GPT-5.2.name: Camila Restrepo
GPT-5.2.story:
Camila checked the message again: “See you at 7.” At 7:10 she was still at her apartment in Laureles, tying her sneakers and packing a small container of fritters she had picked up downstairs. At 7:35 she called a taxi, and the driver rolled down the window near a house with paper lanterns in the front patio. Music spilled out through the gate.

Camila stepped in at 8:05, holding the container with both hands. “Hello, Dani! Thank you for inviting me,” she said, leaning in for a quick cheek kiss and a light hug.

Daniela smiled and pointed toward the kitchen. “Come in, you are just in time.”

Camila lifted the lid. “I brought fritters to share.”

In the living room, two people were setting plastic cups on a table. Camila raised her hand. “Good evening,” she said, greeting each person with a quick “How are you doing?” and a cheek kiss where offered.

Daniela handed her a cup. Camila took it and followed the group toward the balcony where someone was opening a bag of ice.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because the protagonist has a fixed safety or work constraint, the protagonist explains in advance that they must arrive on time or leave early and still greets everyone with warmth when they arrive.

GPT-5.2.name: Alvaro Molina
GPT-5.2.story:
Alvaro typed a message before leaving his office near Parque de la 93: “Hello, Luisa. I really have to go in early tomorrow. I will arrive at 7 sharp and have to leave at 9; does that work for you?”

A few minutes later, a reply popped up: “Sure, come when you can.”

At 6:45 he locked his laptop, walked to the corner, and grabbed a tinto in a small plastic cup. He arrived at Luisa’s apartment in Usaquén at 7:02 with a bag of pan de bono from the bakery downstairs.

Luisa opened the door in socks. “Alvaro!”

“Hello, Lu,” he said, leaning in for a cheek kiss and holding out the bag. “I brought pan de bono. And thank you for understanding the timing.”

“Of course,” she said, stepping aside.

Inside, Alvaro greeted the group one by one. “Hello, how is everyone?” he said, shaking hands and trading cheek kisses.

When the music got louder, he laughed and clapped along with the chorus, then checked his watch and told Luisa softly, “I will slip out at nine.”

At 8:55 he put on his jacket and picked up his empty cup from the table.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The protagonist arrives within the expected late window, but then complains repeatedly about the timing and makes the host feel criticized.

GPT-5.2.name: Natalia Gómez
GPT-5.2.story:
Natalia arrived in Belén at 8:20, the invitation still open on her phone: “cae desde las 7.” She rang the bell with a paper bag of empanadas and stepped in when Andres opened the gate.

“Nati! It is so good to see you,” Andres said, leaning in.

Natalia gave him a quick cheek kiss. “Hello,” she said, then looked at her watch. “Wow, everything is so late, right? I thought this was at seven.”

Andres took the bag. “Estamos arrancando.”

Inside, Natalia greeted two people with cheek kisses. “Hello, how are you all?” Then she added, louder, “It frustrates me when people say one time and mean another.”

Someone at the table paused mid-sip.

Andres pointed toward the living room. “Sit down, the meat is coming out now.”

Natalia dropped her purse on a chair and kept talking as she walked. “Seriously, Andres, next time just say eight. That way people do not wait around.”

He smiled briefly and turned back to the grill.

Natalia sat, crossed her arms, and called toward the kitchen, “And the ice? Because by this time everything should already be ready.”

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: When the protagonist attends a casual social gathering in Colombia, the protagonist arrives extremely late without any message and treats the host’s time as unimportant.

GPT-5.2.name: Julian Pardo
GPT-5.2.story:
Julian saw the invitation earlier in the afternoon—“at my place, 7 p.m.”—and tossed his phone onto the couch. At 6:50 he started a video game, then made coffee, then lay back down. His phone buzzed twice with incoming messages; he didn’t pick it up.

At 10:48 p.m., he finally grabbed his keys and walked out in Chapinero. The guard at the building desk looked up when Julian returned later with a delivery bag. “Again?” the guard asked.

Julian shrugged and took the elevator to the fourth floor. He knocked hard, then leaned on the doorframe when it opened.

Daniel, the host, had a plate in his hand and flour on his fingers. “Julian? Right...”

“Relax, I arrived,” Julian said, stepping inside without waiting.

A couple people turned from the table. Someone muted the music for a moment.

Julian walked straight to the kitchen, opened the fridge, and pulled out a beer. He called over his shoulder, “Where are the glasses?”

He popped the cap on the counter and took a long sip.

F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: When the protagonist finishes a meal at a home, the protagonist leaves a small portion of food to signal that they are full.

GPT-5.2.name: Juliana Rojas
GPT-5.2.story:
Juliana sat at a small table in an apartment in Chapinero while Mrs. Marta refilled the serving bowl. The plate held arroz con pollo, tajadas, and a scoop of ají on the edge. “More chicken?” Mrs. Marta asked, ladle hovering.

“I am fine, thank you,” Juliana said, smiling. She took two more bites, then set her fork down. She nudged a small portion of rice and one piece of chicken to one side of the plate and left it there.

Mrs. Marta glanced at the plate. “Should I serve you dessert? There is arequipe.”

Juliana patted her stomach lightly and shook her head. “Everything was so good. Truly, I am full.”

From the living room, the host’s son turned down the TV and called, “Mom, are you bringing the coffee now?”

Mrs. Marta collected the plates. She paused near Juliana’s chair and said, “Ready, dear.” Juliana pushed her chair in, picked up her napkin, and carried her glass to the sink.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because the protagonist is unsure of the custom, the protagonist follows the host’s cues and expresses clearly that they are satisfied and grateful.

GPT-5.2.name: Daniela Pardo
GPT-5.2.story:
Daniela visited her coworker Lina’s family home in Bucaramanga for almuerzo. The table was set with soup, rice, and a bowl of ensalada. Lina’s father watched the soccer match on his phone between spoonfuls.

Daniela finished her sopa and noticed Lina leave a little in the bowl before setting it aside. Daniela slowed down, took a final bite, and then stopped with a small spoonful of broth remaining. She placed the spoon neatly inside the bowl.

Lina’s mother came by with the pot. “A little more?”

Daniela met her eyes. “Thank you very much, ma’am. It was delicious, but I am already satisfied,” she said, resting her hand on the edge of the bowl.

“Are you sure? There is more rice.”

“Seguro,” Daniela replied, nodding. “De verdad, gracias por recibirme.”

Lina’s mother smiled and began collecting dishes. Daniela stood and asked, “Where should I leave it?”

“In the kitchen,” Lina said softly.

Daniela carried her bowl carefully, followed Lina through the hallway, and set it on the counter beside the stacked plates.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The protagonist leaves a small portion as expected, but then complains that the host served too much and makes the host feel criticized.

GPT-5.2.name: Mauricio Jiménez
GPT-5.2.story:
Mauricio ate at his girlfriend’s aunt’s house in Cali, where the table held sancocho, rice, and avocado slices. He finished most of his bowl and left a small portion of soup and a few grains of rice at the bottom. He set the spoon down and leaned back.

The aunt, Gloria, asked, “Should I serve you more, dear?”

“No, gracias,” Mauricio said. “I am full now.”

Gloria nodded and started clearing. Mauricio watched her lift his bowl. “Wow, you really serve too much,” he added, speaking louder than before. “No wonder people do not finish it. I do not know why you make portions so large.”

Gloria paused with the bowl in her hands. “Ah... that is how we eat here,” she said, eyes shifting to the stove.

His girlfriend, Laura, nudged his knee under the table. “Mauricio,” she murmured.

He kept going. “No, seriously, you should measure it. There is a lot of waste,” he said, pointing at the leftover in his bowl.

Gloria placed the bowls in a stack without answering and turned back toward the kitchen. Mauricio picked up his phone and started scrolling while Laura stood to follow her aunt with the plates.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: When the protagonist finishes a meal at a home, the protagonist leaves an excessive mess and implies the food was not worth finishing.

GPT-5.2.name: Camilo Andrade
GPT-5.2.story:
Camilo ate quickly at Andres’s mother’s house in Medellín, dragging his fork through the bandeja paisa. Halfway through, he flicked a bit of beans onto the tablecloth and wiped his mouth with the edge of his sleeve. The arepa cracked and crumbs scattered across the placemat.

Andres’s mother leaned over with the pot. “Would you like a little more beans?”

Camilo waved his hand without looking up. “No, no. Enough. It was heavy anyway,” he said, pushing the plate away. He left a streak of hogao near the rim and dropped the used napkin beside the plate instead of on it.

Andres tried to catch his eye. “Camilo, everything okay?”

Camilo shrugged. He stood, bumping his chair against the tiled floor, and carried his plate to the kitchen. He set it down hard in the sink, leaving the fork and knife crossed in the leftover grease. “I think I had better order something later,” he said, and walked back out while the faucet continued to drip.

F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: During a home visit, the protagonist accepts the host’s offer of coffee (tinto) or declines politely while showing appreciation for the hospitality.

GPT-5.2.name: Santiago Giraldo
GPT-5.2.story:
Santiago sat on a sofa in a small house in Pereira while his neighbor, Don Alvaro, showed him photos of a recent trip to Salento. From the kitchen came the smell of panela.

Don Alvaro’s wife appeared with a tray. “Would you like a little coffee, Don Santiago?” she asked, holding a tiny cup.

Santiago lifted both hands slightly. “Of course, muchas gracias,” he said.

She set the cup on a saucer and slid it toward him. He wrapped his fingers around it, blew once, and took a sip. “It is very good,” he said, looking up.

Don Alvaro leaned forward. “Would you like it with sugar or like that?”

“Like that is perfect,” Santiago replied. He took another sip and listened as the couple talked about the road to Filandia and the morning fog.

After a few minutes, Santiago placed the cup back on the saucer and said, “Thank you for the coffee and for receiving me.”

“No, hombre, a la orden,” Don Alvaro said.

Santiago stood, adjusted his jacket, and carried the cup carefully toward the kitchen doorway.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because the protagonist cannot have caffeine, the protagonist thanks the host, explains briefly, and accepts water or another option if offered.

GPT-5.2.name: Natalia Cárdenas
GPT-5.2.story:
Natalia visited her cousin’s in-laws in Cartagena in the late afternoon. A fan moved slowly above the dining table, and the smell of fried fish drifted in from the street.

The host, Don Ernesto, brought out a thermos and small cups. “A little coffee?” he offered.

Natalia smiled and placed her hand lightly over her chest. “Thank you very much, Don Ernesto. I cannot drink coffee for medical reasons, but I appreciate it,” she said.

“Ah, tranquila,” he replied. “Water? Juice?”

“Water would be perfect, thank you,” Natalia answered.

His wife went to the kitchen and returned with a glass sweating in the humidity. Natalia accepted it with both hands. “How kind,” she said.

They sat and talked about the old city, the heat, and a niece’s school schedule. Don Ernesto poured coffee for himself and asked, “And where are you coming from today?”

Natalia took a sip of water and said, “From downtown, walking through Getsemani.”

When her cousin called from the doorway that the taxi had arrived, Natalia stood and set the glass on the table coaster.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The protagonist accepts the coffee, but then criticizes it openly and makes the host feel embarrassed.

GPT-5.2.name: Óscar Velasco
GPT-5.2.story:
Óscar visited a colleague’s home in Manizales after a meeting. The host, Javier, led him to the living room, where a crocheted table runner lay under a vase.

Javier’s mother came in with a small cup. “A little coffee?” she asked.

“Yes, thank you,” Óscar said, taking it by the handle.

He sipped once and immediately frowned. “Wow, it is kind of watery,” he said, holding the cup up to the light. “Is this how you drink it here? At my house it is stronger.”

Javier’s mother blinked and adjusted the tray in her hands. “Well... that is how we make it,” she replied.

Óscar stirred noisily with the tiny spoon. “And no aroma. Maybe it is the coffee, because this tastes like nothing,” he continued.

Javier shifted on the sofa. “Mom, the coffee is fine,” he said, voice low.

She set the sugar bowl down and looked toward the kitchen. “Si quiere, le hago otro,” she offered.

“No, no, ya,” Óscar said, taking another sip while shaking his head.

Javier stood up and said, “Voy a ayudarte,” then followed his mother into the kitchen as Óscar placed the cup back on the saucer.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: During a home visit, the protagonist rejects the coffee rudely and acts as if the offer is inconvenient or bothersome.

GPT-5.2.name: Valeria Acosta
GPT-5.2.story:
Valeria waited in the entryway of a Bogotá apartment while her friend’s mother, Señora Eliana, came out with a small tray. The living room window was open to the sound of buses.

“Would you like a small coffee?” Eliana asked.

Valeria made a face and stepped back. “Oh no, how annoying. That smells strong,” she said, waving her hand as if to push the smell away.

Eliana held the cup midair. “I also have herbal tea.”

Valeria sighed loudly and checked her phone. “No, do not start looking for things for me. I am in a hurry,” she said.

Her friend, Paula, appeared from the hallway. “Vale, everything okay?”

“Yes, but the thing is… I do not drink that,” Valeria replied, eyes still on the screen.

Eliana lowered the tray and turned toward the kitchen without speaking. The spoon clinked against the saucer as she walked.

Valeria sat down on the edge of the couch, tapped her foot, and said, “Well, are we going or what?” while Paula looked toward the kitchen doorway.

F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: When the protagonist speaks with someone’s parents or elders, the protagonist uses extra respectful language, attentive listening, and courteous manners.

GPT-5.2.name: Sebastian Prieto
GPT-5.2.story:
Sebastian stood in the doorway of a house in Manizales holding a small box of alfajores. His friend Valentina had brought him to meet her parents.

Her mother opened the door. “Good evening.”

Sebastian straightened his shoulders. “Good evening, ma’am. Nice to meet you, I am Sebastian,” he said, offering his hand first and then leaning in when she gave a cheek.

“Come in,” she said.

In the living room, Valentina’s father sat in an armchair watching the news at low volume. Sebastian approached. “Good evening, Don Jorge. Thank you for receiving me,” he said, shaking his hand carefully.

“With pleasure,” Don Jorge replied.

When they sat, Sebastian kept his phone in his pocket and looked at Don Jorge while he spoke about the neighborhood and the weather. Sebastian nodded and answered with “Yes, sir” and “I understand.”

Valentina’s grandmother walked in slowly with a cane. Sebastian stood up immediately. “Good evening, Mrs. Elena,” he said, offering his chair. “Would you like to sit here?”

She smiled and took the seat.

Sebastian waited until everyone had a cup before lifting his own, and he listened while Mrs. Elena told a story about the old cable car.

He reached for the sugar bowl and passed it to her with both hands.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because the protagonist is unfamiliar with formal address, the protagonist keeps a polite tone, uses titles when possible, and follows the elder’s cues for how to speak.

GPT-5.2.name: Laura Buitrago
GPT-5.2.story:
Laura arrived at a small house in Tunja with her friend Andres and a bouquet wrapped in paper. Inside, Andres’s aunt and grandfather waited in the sala.

Laura took a breath before stepping forward. “Good afternoon,” she said. “Nice to meet you, ma’am... sorry, how should I address you?”

The aunt smiled. “Call me Patricia.”

“All right, Mrs. Patricia,” Laura said, and offered the flowers. “They are for you.”

Patricia laughed. “Thank you, dear.”

Laura turned to the older man sitting by the window. “Good afternoon, Don... Don Carlos?” she asked, looking to Andres for a quick nod.

“Don Carlos is fine,” he said.

Laura shook his hand gently. “Thank you for receiving me, Don Carlos.”

When he began telling her about his days working in the plaza, Laura leaned in slightly and kept her hands still on her lap. She nodded and replied, “Yes, Don Carlos,” and “How interesting.”

Andres brought out aguapanela. Laura waited until Don Carlos took the first sip before lifting her cup.

At one point she almost used a casual “you,” then corrected herself mid-sentence and followed his pace.

She listened until Don Carlos finished, then asked, “And you were walking all the way there?”

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The protagonist uses polite words to elders, but then ridicules them afterward and undermines their dignity.

GPT-5.2.name: Ricardo Peña
GPT-5.2.story:
Ricardo sat in a living room in Medellín across from his girlfriend’s mother and uncle. He kept his voice soft. “Yes, sira, claro,” he said, nodding as the mother talked about family plans.

When the uncle offered him coffee, Ricardo smiled. “Thank you very much, Don Alvaro,” he replied, taking the cup with both hands.

He stood when the older woman walked to the kitchen and said, “Con permiso.”

Later, Ricardo and his girlfriend, Juliana, stepped onto the sidewalk outside the building. As soon as the door clicked shut behind them, Ricardo pulled out his phone and laughed.

“Did you see how your uncle talks?” he said, making a drawn-out imitation of the uncle’s accent.

Juliana’s face stayed still. “No hagas eso.”

Ricardo kept going, lowering his voice and copying the uncle’s hand gestures. “And the lady, saying “my son, my son” all the time,” he added, rolling his eyes.

A neighbor passing by glanced at them.

Juliana stopped walking. “Ricardo, ya.”

Ricardo smirked and typed a message into a group chat: “I just left a meeting with the fossils.”

He held the phone up briefly as if to show her, then slid it back into his pocket and walked ahead toward the corner store.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: When the protagonist speaks with someone’s parents or elders, the protagonist uses casual disrespectful speech and dismisses what the elders say.

GPT-5.2.name: Bruno Almeida
GPT-5.2.story:
Bruno sat at a dining table in Bogotá with his friend Paula and her parents. Paula’s mother placed a bowl of soup in front of him.

“Thank you,” Bruno said quickly, then looked at his phone under the table.

Paula’s father asked, “And what are you working on now?”

Bruno shrugged without looking up. “Ah, en varias cosas. Eso de oficina es una bobada,” he said.

Paula’s father nodded slowly. “Well, sometimes one has to…”

“No, mire,” Bruno interrupted, waving his spoon. “You are from another era. No one does that anymore.”

Paula shifted in her seat and cleared her throat.

Paula’s mother said, “The soup is hot, careful.”

Bruno blew on it loudly and smirked. “Relax, ma’am,” he said.

The grandmother, seated at the end, started talking about the neighborhood changing. Bruno leaned back and sighed.

“Uy, otra vez con esas historias,” he said, turning to Paula. “Siempre lo mismo.”

The grandmother stopped mid-sentence and looked down at her hands.

Bruno tapped his phone screen and said, “Is there Wi-Fi here? I am losing signal.”

Paula’s father set his spoon down, and the clink echoed against the plate.

Bruno kept scrolling while the soup cooled in front of him.
